comments.text: Hello, I’ve been playing with Erdős Problem #885 in my spare time because I found it fun, and I wanted to share two partial results that seemed worth recording. Disclosure: I used AI-assisted search, reformulation, computation, and pattern testing during exploration. I then consolidated the useful parts, formalized the posted statements in Lean with Aristotle, and checked the claims myself. I am not claiming a solution to #885; I am only posting two partial findings that, to the best of my literature search, I have not found recorded in the published #885 literature or on the Erdős Problems forum. Repo: https://github.com/SamMausberg/lean-formalizations/tree/main/FormalConjectures/Problems/Erdos/E885/ForumNote PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1EkIEg14iQezGVNVBXSEfm7Q8ar3af2Jk/view?usp=sharing I am not claiming a solution of E885 itself, only these two formalized partial results. As suggested, I wanted to put the claims from the PDF in this comment.The note contains two separate claims: First, if\[ Y(a,b,c)=\{\, z>0 : z^2+a,\ z^2+b,\ z^2+c \text{ are all squares} \,\}, \]then\[ \{330,870,2445,4155,10482\}\subseteq Y(756000,15971200,45130176), \]so\[ \bigl|Y(756000,15971200,45130176)\bigr| \ge 5. \]In particular, the universal anchored bound\[ |Y(a,b,c)| \le 4 \]is false. Second, and more directly in the language of E885, I prove the exact finite computation\[ D(79200)\cap D(227205)\cap D(1258560)=\{36,468,692,1028\}. \]

Thanks; it would be useful if you could also include in the comment a (brief) summary of what the results you're claiming are. In this case it seems to be the computational example\[D(79200)\cap D(227205)\cap D(1258560) = \{ 36,468,692,1029\}.\]
